entries.title: Customizing Burp's layout: overview
entries.body:
# Customizing Burp's layout

Burp's interface does not have to stay the way it opened by default. The layout can be tailored to your workflow: rearrange and hide tabs, move tools out into separate windows, reconfigure tables. Burp remembers your preferences between runs - set it up once, and at the next start everything is in its place.

What can be customized:

- **Top-level tabs** (the top tabs: **Dashboard**, **Target**, **Proxy**, **Intruder**, **Repeater**, etc.) - order, hiding, moving out into separate windows.
- **Tables** (the tables in many tools - **Proxy** history, **Logger**, **Site map**, etc.) - which columns to show, in what order, sorting, filters, and in Pro also custom columns.

Separately from the layout, the appearance is configured in **Settings > UI** - that is where the theme (light/dark), fonts and other visual parameters are. This is not about the placement of elements, but about styling.

```
What we tweak:
  Top-level tabs -> order / hide / detach into a window
  Tables         -> columns / sorting / filter / custom columns (Pro)
  Settings > UI  -> theme, fonts (appearance, not layout)
```

> Tip: the point of customization is not beauty, but speed. Hide the tabs you do not use, move **Repeater** to a second monitor, remove unnecessary columns from the **Proxy** history - and it becomes noticeably easier on the eyes to find what you need in dense traffic.

Next - two detailed topics: customizing the top tabs and customizing tables.

entries.title: Customizing the top-level tabs
entries.body:
# Customizing the top-level tabs

The top-level tabs are Burp's main navigation: **Dashboard**, **Target**, **Proxy**, **Intruder**, **Repeater**, **Collaborator**, **Sequencer** and the rest. They can be rearranged, hidden and moved out into separate windows. Burp saves all changes between sessions.

## Change the order of tabs

Click a tab and, holding it, drag it along the tab bar to the place you need. Just horizontal drag-and-drop.

## Detach a tab into a separate window

Right-click a tab -> **Detach tab**. The tab moves into its own window. This is especially handy with two monitors: for example, **Repeater** or **Proxy** can be moved to a second screen and worked on in parallel.

You can also drag a tab into an ALREADY existing separate window: grab the tab and drop it onto that window's tab bar.

## Return a tab to its place

- Right-click a detached tab -> **Reattach tab** - return a specific tab to the main window.
- Or, in the detached window, click the redock icon - it returns all of that window's tabs back.
- If you simply close the separate window - all of its tabs will be HIDDEN (not deleted).

## Hide a tab

Right-click a tab -> **Hide tab**. The tab disappears from the bar. Useful for removing what you do not use (for example, **Sequencer** or **Comparer**, if you do not need them right now), to make navigation more compact.

## Show hidden tabs

Hidden tabs live in the top **View** menu: they are listed there and marked with a special icon. To bring back a hidden tab - open the **View** menu and click its name; the tab returns to its former place.

> Useful behavior: if you send a request or response to a hidden tab (for example, **Send to Repeater** when **Repeater** is hidden), that tab automatically reappears. So you cannot accidentally "lose" a tool by hiding it.

## Reset the tab layout

The top menu **View > Restore default tab layout** - returns all tabs to their default places and shows all hidden ones. A quick way to roll back layout experiments in one action.

```
Right-click a tab:
  Detach tab    -> into a separate window
  Reattach tab  -> return to the main window
  Hide tab      -> hide (bring back via the View menu)

View menu:
  <tab name> -> show a hidden one
  Restore default tab layout -> reset everything
```

> Two-monitor tip: move **Proxy > HTTP history** to one screen and **Repeater** to another via **Detach tab**. Triaging traffic and manually fine-tuning requests will stop overlapping each other, and switching between them becomes unnecessary.

entries.title: Customizing tables
entries.body:
# Customizing tables

Many of Burp's screens are tables: the **Proxy > HTTP history**, **Logger**, the site map **Target > Site map**, lists of findings, etc. Their layout can be tailored to you: hide unnecessary columns, rearrange them, sort and filter the data. Burp remembers all of this between runs.

## Hide a column

Right-click a column header -> **Hide column**. The unnecessary column disappears. Handy for removing what you never look at, so that in a dense table the important stuff does not get pushed off the right edge of the screen.

## Show hidden columns

Open the table's options menu -> **Table layout** and check the columns you want to see. The same place has a full list of available columns with checkboxes.

## Rearrange columns

Drag a column header (drag-and-drop) to a new place. This lets you move the most needed columns to the left, closer to the start.

## Sorting

Clicking a column header cycles the sorting: ascending -> descending -> no sorting. For example, in the **Proxy** history it is handy to sort by status code or by response length to quickly find anomalies.

## Filtering

Click the filter settings bar above the table -> set the criteria (by content type, by response code, by extension, by whether it is in scope or not, etc.). The table will keep only the matching rows. This is the main way to pick out what you need from thousands of traffic entries.

## Copying column data (Pro)

Right-click a column header -> **Copy column** - copies the values of the whole column to the clipboard. Handy for exporting, say, the list of all URLs or all status codes in bulk for further processing.

## Reset the table

Options menu -> **Table layout > Restore default table** - returns the columns to their default view, if you have experimented too much.

```
Column header (right-click):
  Hide column   -> hide the column
  Copy column   -> copy the values (Pro)

Table options menu (Table layout):
  [v] show/hide specific columns
  Restore default table -> reset

Filter settings bar -> filter rows by criteria
```

## Advanced: Bambda and custom columns (Pro)

In Professional, tables can do more:

- **Add custom column** (in the options menu) - add your own column whose value is computed by a script. For example, output the value of a needed header or a flag based on your own condition in a separate column.
- **Script mode in the filter** - switch the filter into script mode and write your own selection logic in **Bambda** (Burp's Java-like mini-scripts). This lets you filter by any conditions that are not in the ordinary checkboxes - for example "show only responses that have header X and code 500".

> Tip: the combination of a **Bambda** filter and a custom column turns the **Proxy** history into a powerful analysis tool. On large traffic this lets you pick out exactly the requests you are interested in in seconds, instead of manually leafing through thousands of rows.

> Pitfall: do not get so carried away with hiding columns that you lose sight of the status code, response length and MIME type - these are the three main indicators during triage. Hide the secondary stuff, keep the key stuff in view.
